title: How to define custom workflows
text:
<!-- TODO Possibly update this example, and maybe define the scope of usage -->

The abstract workflow schema above allows us to build generalized tools for workflows,
like workflow searches, navigation in workflow, graphical representations of workflows, etc.

title: How to define custom workflows
text:
But, you can still augment the given section definitions with more information through
inheritance.

title: How to define custom workflows
text:
These information can be specialized references to denote inputs and outputs,
can be additional workflow or task parameters, and much more.

title: How to define custom workflows
text:
In this example, we created a special workflow section definition `GeometryOptimization`
that defines a parameter `threshold` and an additional reference to the final
calculation of the optimization:

```yaml
definitions:
  sections:
    GeometryOptimizationWorkflow:
      base_section: nomad.datamodel.metainfo.workflow.Workflow
      quantities:
        threshold:
          type: float
          unit: eV
        final_calculation:
          type: runschema.calculation.Calculation

workflow2:
  m_def: GeometryOptimizationWorkflow
  final_calculation: '#/run/0/calculation/-1'
  threshold: 0.029
  name: GeometryOpt
  inputs:
    ...
```

title: How to navigate the code
text: NOMAD is a complex project with lots of parts.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
This guide gives you a rough overview
about the codebase and ideas about what to look at first.

title: How to navigate the code
How to navigate the code
text:
This project contains all the framework and infrastructure code. It instigates all checks,
builds, and deployments for the public NOMAD service, the NOMAD Oasis, and the `nomad-lab`
Python package. All contributions to NOMAD have to go through this project eventually. All (Git) projects that NOMAD depends on are either a Git submodule (you find
them all in the `dependencies` directory or its subdirectories) or they are
listed as PyPI packages in the `pyproject.toml` of the main project (or one of its
submodules). You can also have a look at the [built-in plugins](../../reference/plugins.md) that
constitute the majority of these projects. The only other projects are
[MatID](https://github.com/nomad-coe/matid){:target="_blank"},
[DOS fingerprints](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-dos-fingerprints){:target="_blank"},
and the
[NOMAD Remote Tools Hub](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-remote-tools-hub){:target="_blank"}.

!!! note
    The GitLab organization
    [nomad-lab](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab){:target="_blank"} and the GitHub
    organizations for [FAIRmat](https://github.com/fairmat-nfdi){:target="_blank"} and the
    [NOMAD CoE](https://github.com/nomad-coe){:target="_blank"} all represent larger
    infrastructure and research projects, and they include many other Git projects that
    are not related. When navigating the codebase, only follow the submodules.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
There are three main directories with Python code:

- `nomad`: The actual NOMAD code.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
It is structured into more subdirectories and modules.

- `tests`: Tests ([pytest](https://docs.pytest.org){:target="_blank"}) for the NOMAD code.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
It follows the same module structure, but Python files are prefixed with `test_`.

- `examples`: A few small Python scripts that might be linked in the documentation.

title: How to navigate the code
text: The `nomad` directory contains the following "main" modules.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
This list is not extensive
but should help you to navigate the codebase:

- `app`: The [FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/){:target="_blank"} APIs: v1 and v1.2
  NOMAD APIs,
  [OPTIMADE](https://www.optimade.org/){:target="_blank"},
  [DCAT](https://www.w3.org/TR/vocab-dcat-2/){:target="_blank"},
  [h5grove](https://github.com/silx-kit/h5grove){:target="_blank"}, and more.

- `archive`: Functionality to store and access archive files.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
This is the storage format
  for all processed data in NOMAD.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
See also the docs on
  [structured data](../../explanation/data.md).

- `cli`: The command line interface (based on
  [Click](https://click.palletsprojects.com){:target="_blank"}).

title: How to navigate the code
text:
Subcommands are
  structured into submodules.

- `config`: NOMAD is configured through the `nomad.yaml` file.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
This contains all the
  ([Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/){:target="_blank"}) models and default config
  parameters.

- `datamodel`: The built-in schemas (e.g. `nomad.datamodel.metainfo.workflow` used to
  construct workflows).

title: How to navigate the code
text:
The base sections and section for the shared entry structure. See also the docs on the [datamodel](../../explanation/data.md) and
  [processing](../../explanation/basics.md).

- `metainfo`: The Metainfo system, e.g. the schema language that NOMAD uses.

- `normalizing`: All the normalizers. See also the docs on
  [processing](../../explanation/basics.md#normalizing).

- `parsing`: The base classes for parsers, matching functionality, parser initialization,
  some fundamental parsers like the *archive* parser. See also the docs on
  [processing](../../explanation/basics.md#parsing).

- `processing`: It's all about processing uploads and entries. The interface to
  [Celery](https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/stable/){:target="_blank"} and
  [MongoDB](https://www.mongodb.com).

- `units`: The unit and unit conversion system based on
  [Pint](https://pint.readthedocs.io){:target="_blank"}.

- `utils`: Utility modules, e.g. the structured logging system
  ([structlog](https://www.structlog.org/){:target="_blank"}), id generation, and hashes.

- `files.py`: Functionality to maintain the files for uploads in staging and published. The interface to the file system.

- `search.py`: The interface to
  [Elasticsearch](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/enterprise-search/current/start.html){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
The NOMAD UI is written as a [React](https://react.dev/){:target="_blank"} single-page
application (SPA). It uses (among many other libraries)
[MUI](https://mui.com/){:target="_blank"},
[Plotly](https://plotly.com/python/){:target="_blank"}, and
[D3](https://d3js.org/){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
The GUI code is maintained in the `gui`
directory. Most relevant code can be found in `gui/src/components`. The application entry
point is `gui/src/index.js`.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
The documentation is based on [MkDocs](https://www.mkdocs.org/){:target="_blank"}. The
important files and directories are:

- `docs`: Contains all the Markdown files that contribute to the documentation system.

- `mkdocs.yml`: The index and configuration of the documentation.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
New files have to be
  added here as well.

- `nomad/mkdocs.py`: Python code that defines
  [macros](https://mkdocs-macros-plugin.readthedocs.io/){:target="_blank"} which can be
  used in Markdown.

title: How to navigate the code
text:
- `dependencies`: Contains all the submodules, e.g. the parsers.

- `ops`: Contains artifacts to run NOMAD components, e.g. `docker-compose.yaml` files,
  and our Kubernetes Helm chart.

- `scripts`: Contains scripts used during the build or for certain development tasks.

title: How to contribute
text:
!!! note

    The NOMAD source code is maintained in two synchronized projects on
    [GitHub](https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad){:target="_blank"} and a
    [GitLab run by MPCDF](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to contribute
text: Everyone can contribute on GitHub.

title: How to contribute
text:
The GitLab instance requires an account for active
    contribution.

title: How to contribute
text:
This not an ideal situation: there are historic reasons and there is
    a lot of buy-in into the GitLab CI/CD system.

title: How to contribute
text:
This guide addresses contributions
    to both projects.

title: How to contribute
text:
Everyone can open a [new issue](https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad/issues/new){:target="_blank"} in our main
[GitHub project](https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to contribute
text: Use issues to ask questions, report bugs, or suggest features.

title: How to contribute
text:
If in doubt, use the main
project to engage with us.

title: How to contribute
text:
If you address a specific plugin (e.g. parser), you can also
post into the respective projects.

title: How to contribute
text: See also the list of [built-in plugins](../../reference/plugins.md).

title: How to contribute
text:
If you are a member of FAIRmat, the NOMAD CoE, or are a close collaborator, you
probably have an MPCDF GitLab account (or should ask us for one).

title: How to contribute
text:
Please use the
issue tracker on our main
[GitLab project](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to contribute
text: This is where most of the implementation work is planned and executed.

title: How to contribute
text:
A few tips that will help us to solve your issues quicker:

- Focus on the issue.

title: How to contribute
text: You don't need to greet us or say thanks and goodbye.

title: How to contribute
text:
Let's keep it
  technical.

- Use descriptive short issue titles.

title: How to contribute
text:
Use specific words over general words.

- Describe the observed problem and not the assumed causes.

title: How to contribute
text:
Clearly separate speculation
  from the problem description.

- **Bugs**: Think how we could reproduce the problem:

    - What NOMAD URL are you using (UI), which package version (Python)?
    - Is there an upload or entry id that we can look at?
    - Example files or code snippets?
    - Don't screenshot code, copy and paste instead.

title: How to contribute
text:
Use [code blocks](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/writing-on-github/working-with-advanced-formatting/creating-and-highlighting-code-blocks#syntax-highlighting){:target="_blank"}.

- **Features**: Augment your feature descriptions with a use case that helps us understand the feature and its scope.

title: How to contribute
text: On the main GitLab project, there are three main categories for labels.

title: How to contribute
text:
Ideally, each
issue gets one of each category:

- *State* label (grey): These are used to manage when and how the issue is addressed.

title: How to contribute
text:
This
should be given by the NOMAD team member who is currently responsible to moderate the
development.

title: How to contribute
text:
If it's a simple fix and you want to do it yourself, assign yourself and use
"bugfixes".

- *Component* label (purple): These denote the part of the NOMAD software that is most
likely effected.

title: How to contribute
text:
Multiple purple labels are possible.

- *Kind* label (red): Whether this is a bug, feature, refactoring, or documentation issue.

title: How to contribute
text: Unlabeled issues will get labeled by the NOMAD team as soon as possible.

title: How to contribute
text:
You can provide
labels yourself.

title: How to contribute
text: The documentation is part of NOMAD's main source code project.

title: How to contribute
text:
You can
[raise issues](#issues) about the documentation as usual.

title: How to contribute
text:
To make changes, create a
[merge](#merge-requests-mr-gitlab) or [pull](#pull-requests-pr-github) request.

title: How to contribute
text: See also the [documentation part](./code.md#documentation) in our code navigation guide.

title: How to contribute
text: Also read the guide on [how to develop, publish, and distribute plugins](../plugins/plugins.md).

title: How to contribute
text:
Most plugins that are maintained by the NOMAD team are built-in plugins (e.g. all the
parsers). These plugins are also available on the public NOMAD service. These plugins are tied to the main project's source code via dependencies. To contribute to these plugins, use the respective GitHub projects. See also the
list of [built-in plugins](../../reference/plugins.md).

title: How to contribute
text:
The same rules apply there.

```shell
git checkout something-with-changes
git status
```

title: How to contribute
text:
Please open an issue, if you want to announce a plugin or contribute a plugin as a
potential built-in plugin (i.e. as part of the public NOMAD service).

title: How to contribute
text:
On the [main GitLab project](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR){:target="_blank"} we use
*protected* and *feature* branches.

title: How to contribute
text:
You must not commit to protected branches directly
(even if you have the rights).

- `develop`: a *protected* branch and the *default* branch.

title: How to contribute
text:
It contains the latest,
  potentially unreleased, features and fixes.

title: How to contribute
text:
This is the main working branch.

- `master`: a *protected* branch.

title: How to contribute
text:
Represents the latest stable release (usually what the
  current official NOMAD runs on).

- *feature branches*: this is where you work.

title: How to contribute
text:
Typically they are automatically (use the
  "Create merge request" button) named after issues: `<issue-number>-<issue-title>`.

- `vX.X.X` or `vX.X.XrcX`: *tags* for (pre-)releases.

title: How to contribute
text:
The `develop` branch and release tags are automatically synchronized to the
[GitHub project](https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad){:target="_blank"}. Otherwise, this project is mostly
the target for [pull requests](#pull-requests-pr-github) and does not contain other relevant
branches.

title: How to contribute
text:
Ideally, have an issue first and create the merge request (and branch) in the GitLab UI. There is a "Create merge request" button on each issue. When done manually, branches
should be based on the `develop` branch and merge request should target `develop` as well.

title: How to contribute
text:
Make sure you follow our [code guidelines](../../reference/code_guidelines.md) and
[set up your IDE](./setup.md#setup-your-ide) to enforce style checks, linting, and static
analysis.

title: How to contribute
text: You can also run [tests locally](./setup.md#running-tests).

title: How to contribute
text:
Try to keep a clean
commit history and follow our [Git tips](#tips-for-a-clean-git-history).

title: How to contribute
text: Usually only one person is working on a feature branch.

title: How to contribute
text:
Rebasing, amendments, and force
pushes are allowed.

title: How to contribute
text: We have an automatically generated changelog in the repository file `CHANGELOG.md`.

title: How to contribute
text:
This changelog is produced from commit messages and to maintain this file, you
need to write commit messages accordingly. To trigger a changelog entry, your commit needs to end with a so-called *Git trailer*
named `Changelog`. A typical commit message for a changelog entry should look like this:

```text
A brief one-line title of the change. A longer *Markdown*-formatted description of the change. Keep in mind that GitLab will
automatically link the changelog entry with this commit and a respective merge requests.

title: How to contribute
text: You do not need to manually link to any GitLab resources.

title: How to contribute
text: This could span multiple paragraphs.

title: How to contribute
text: However, keep it short.

title: How to contribute
text:
Documentation should go into
the actual documentation, but you should mention breaks in backward compatibility,
deprecation of features, etc. Changelog: Fixed
```

The trailer value (`Fixed` in the example) has to be one of the following values:

- `Fixed` for bugfixes.

- `Added` for new features.

- `Changed` for general improvements, e.g. updated documentation, refactoring,
improving performance, etc.

title: How to contribute
text: These categories are consistent with [keepachangelog.com](https://keepachangelog.com/){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to contribute
text:
For more information about the changelog generation read the
[GitLab documentation](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/repositories.html#add-changelog-data-to-a-changelog-file){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to contribute
text: If you push to your merge requests, GitLab will run an extensive CI/CD pipeline.

title: How to contribute
text:
You need to pay attention to failures and resolve them before review. To review GUI changes, you can deploy your branch to the dev cluster via CI/CD actions. Find someone on the NOMAD developer team to review your merge request and request a review
through GitLab. The review should be performed shortly and should not stall the merge
request longer than two full work days. The reviewer needs to be able to learn about the merge request and what it tries to
achieve. This information can come from:

- the linked issue
- the merge request description (may contain your commit message) and your comments
- threads that were opened by the author to attach comments to specific places in the code

The reviewer will open *threads* that need to be solved by the merge request author.

title: How to contribute
text:
If
all threads are resolved, you can request another review.

title: How to contribute
text:
For complex merge requests, you can also comment your code and create *threads* for
the reviewer to resolve.

title: How to contribute
text: This will allow you to explain your changes.

title: How to contribute
text:
The branch should be recently rebased with `develop` to allow a smooth merge:

```shell
git fetch
git rebase origin/develop
git push origin <branch> -f
```

The merge is usually performed by the merge request author after a positive review. If you could not keep a clean Git history with your commits, squash the merge request.

title: How to contribute
text:
Make sure to edit the commit message when squashing to have a [changelog](#changelog)
entry. Make sure to delete the branch on merge. The respective issue usually closes itself, due
to the references put in by GitLab.

title: How to contribute
text:
You can fork the [main NOMAD project](https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad){:target="_blank"} and create pull
requests following the usual GitHub flow.

title: How to contribute
text:
Make sure to target the `develop` branch. A team
member will pick up your pull request and automatically copy it to GitLab to run the
pipeline and potentially perform the merge.

title: How to contribute
text:
This process is made transparent in the pull
request discussion. Your commits and your authorship is maintained in this process.

title: How to contribute
text: Similar rules apply to all the parser and plugin projects.

title: How to contribute
text:
Here, pipelines are run
directly on GitHub.

title: How to contribute
text: A team member will review and potentially merge your pull requests.

title: How to contribute
section: Tips for a clean Git history
text:
It is often necessary to consider code history to reason about potential problems in our
code.

title: How to contribute
section: Tips for a clean Git history
text:
This can only be done if we keep a "clean" history.

- Use descriptive commit messages.

title: How to contribute
section: Tips for a clean Git history
text:
Use simple verbs (*added*, *removed*, *refactored*,
  etc.) name features and changed components.
  [Include issue numbers](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/issues/crosslinking_issues.html){:target="_blank"}
  to create links in GitLab.

- Learn how to *amend* to avoid lists of small related commits.

- Learn how to *rebase*.

title: How to contribute
section: Tips for a clean Git history

title: How to contribute
text:
While working on a feature, there are certain practices that will help us to create a clean history with coherent commits, where each commit stands on its own.

```shell
git commit --amend
```

If you committed something to your own feature branch and then realize by CI that you have some tiny error in it you need to fix, try to amend this fix to the last commit.

title: How to contribute
text: This will avoid unnecessary tiny commits and foster more coherent single commits.

title: How to contribute
text: With `--amend` you are adding changes to the last commit, i.e. editing the last commit.

title: How to contribute
text: When you push, you need to force it, e.g. `git push origin feature-branch --force-with-lease`.

title: How to contribute
text: So be careful, and only use this on your own branches.

title: How to contribute
text:
```shell
git rebase <version-branch>
```

Let's assume you work on a bigger feature that takes more time. You might want to merge the version branch into your feature branch from time to time to get the recent changes.

title: How to contribute
text: In these cases, use `rebase` and not `merge`.

title: How to contribute
text: Rebasing puts your branch commits in front of the merged commits instead of creating a new commit with two ancestors.

title: How to contribute
text: It moves the point where you initially branched away from the version branch to the current position in the version branch.

title: How to contribute
text: This will avoid merges, merge commits, and generally leaves us with a more consistent history.

title: How to contribute
text: You can also rebase before creating a merge request, which allows no-op merges.

title: How to contribute
text: Ideally, the only real merges that we ever have are between version branches.

title: How to contribute
text:
```shell
git merge --squash <other-branch>
```

When you need multiple branches to implement a feature and merge between them, try to use `--squash`.

title: How to contribute
text: Squashing puts all commits of the merged branch into a single commit.

title: How to contribute
text: It allows you to have many commits and then squash them into one.

title: How to contribute
text: This is useful if these commits were made just to synchronize between workstations, due to unexpected errors in CI/CD, because you needed a save point, etc.

title: How to contribute
text: Again the goal is to have coherent commits, where each commit makes sense on its own.

title: How to contribute
text: Squashing can also be applied on a selection of commits during an [interactive rebase](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Rewriting-History#_squashing){:target="_blank"}.

title: Write a normalizer
text:
The root context, which is available from the `.m_context` of a `EntryArchive`, which could be accessed via `section.m_root().m_context` if `section` is attached to a `EntryArchive`, provides the functionality to update/create child entries on-the-fly and invoke the processing if necessary.

!!! note
    The usage of this functionality is strongly discouraged and should be avoided if possible. The method has the following signature.

```python
    @contextmanager
    def update_entry(
        self,
        mainfile: str,
        *,
        write: bool = False,
        process: bool = False,
        **kwargs,
    ):
        """
        Open the target file and send it to the updater function. The updater function shall return the updated file content. The updated file will be stored and processed if needed. WARNING:
            If `process=True`, the updated file will be processed immediately. Please be aware of the fact that this method may be called during the processing of
            the parent/main file.

title: Write a normalizer
text:
This means if there are any data dependencies, there is a risk of infinite loops,
            racing conditions and/or other unexpected behavior.

title: Write a normalizer
text:
You must carefully design the logic to mitigate these risks. To use this function, you shall use the with-statement as follows:

        ```python
        with context.update_entry('mainfile.json',**kwargs) as content:
            # do something with content
        ```

        Parameters:
            mainfile: The relative path (from upload root) to the file to update.
            write: Whether to write the updated file back to the storage. If False, no processing will be triggered whatsoever.
            process: Whether to trigger processing of the updated file.
        """
        ...
```

It is wrapped with a `@contextmanager` decorator, thus it shall be used with a `with` block. It yields a plain `dict` object that represents the content of the file.

```python

title: Write a normalizer
section: get the context from the current archive
text: context = section.m_root().m_context

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text:
with context.update_entry('mainfile.json', process=True) as content_dict:
    # do something with content
    content_dict['key'] = 'value'
    ...
```

The main file must be a `json` or `yaml` file.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text: Other formats are not supported.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text: If only need to read the content, leave `write=False`. Otherwise, set `write=True` to store the updated content back to the storage. It is possible to invoke the processing immediately by setting `process=True`.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text: However, this is not recommended due to various security concerns.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text:
The following caveats must be acknowledged when using this method:

1.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text:
The specific logic of creating/updating the file must be re-entrant safe, see [details](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reentrancy_(computing)). To put simply, the first call and subsequent calls must yield the same result regardless of what is already stored in the file.
2.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text:
A child entry must **not** be accessed by multiple parent entries. Because the parent entries are processed in parallel (by multiple `celery` workers), there is a risk of racing conditions if the child entry is accessed by multiple parent entries.
3. The child entry shall not modify the parent entry (and any other entries).

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text:
Otherwise, there is a risk of infinite loops and data corruption.
4.

title: Write a normalizer
section: create/update the file 'mainfile.json' and process it
text: A child entry shall **not** depend on other child entries.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
NOMAD is a public service, a Git repository, a Python package, and a docker image. What exactly is a NOMAD release? It is all of the following:

- a version tag on the main NOMAD [git project](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR),
e.g. [`v1.3.0`](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR/-/tags/v1.3.0)
- a gitlab release based on a tag with potential release notes
- a version of the `nomad-lab` Python package released to pypi.org, e.g. `nomad-lab==1.3.0`.
- a docker image tag, e.g. `gitlab-registry.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-fair:v1.3.0`
- the docker image tag `stable` points to the image with the latest release tag

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
- Silently create a new version tag in the `v1.3.0` format.
- Deploy the build from this tag to the public NOMAD deployments.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: What deployments are updated might depend on the current needs.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
But usually
the production and test deployment should be updated.
- Release the Python package to the local gitlab registry. (This will update the
NORTH Jupyter image in the next nightly build and most likely effect plugins)
- Bump the `latest` docker image tag.
- For minor and major releases, encourage (Oasis) users to test the public services and the latest docker image for a short trial phase (e.g. 3 days). For patch releases this step should be
skipped.
- Create a gitlab release from the tag with potential release notes. Those notes
should also be added to the README.md. It is ok, if the updated README.md is not part of the
release itself.
- Bump the `stable` docker image tag.
- Publish the Python package to [pypi.org](https://pypi.org/)

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: This depends on the current `develop` branch and requires a judgement call.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
There are
two opposing scenarios:

1.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
The `develop` branch only contains minor fixes or fix/features that are not likely to effect
the released functionality.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
In this case, a new release with an increased patch version
is the right call.

2.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
The `develop` branch adds major refactorings and commits that likely effect the
released functionality.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
In this case, a `v1.3.0-hotfix` branch should be created. After adding commits with the hotfix, the release process can be applied to the
hotfix branch in order to create a `v1.3.1` release that only contains the hotfixes and
not the changes on develop. After the `v1.3.1` release, the `v1.3.0-hotfix` branch is merged
back into develop.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Hotfix branches should not live longer than a week.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: - **patch**: No significant refactorings.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Only new/updated features behind disabled feature switches.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Bugfixes.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Might mark features as deprecated.

- **minor**: Might enabled new features by default.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Can contain major refactorings (especially if they effect to plugin developers, data stewards etc.).

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Might finally deprecate features.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Should "basically" be backwards compatible.

- **major**: Breaking changes and will require data migration. What is a *breaking change* and what does "basically" backwards compatible mean?

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
We develop experimental functionality and often need multiple iterations
to get a feature right.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
This also means that we technically introduce breaking changes
far more often than we can issue major releases.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
It is again a judgement call to decide on
major vs minor.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
The following things would generally not be considered *breaking* and would be considered *backwards compatible*:

- the breaking change is for a feature that is not enabled by default
- data migration is necessary for new functionality, but optional for existing functionality
- it is unlikely that plugins not developed by FAIRmat are effected
- it is unlikely that data beyond the central NOMAD deployments need to be migrated

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: Patch releases should happen frequently and at least once every other month.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Also
minor releases should be done semi regular.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Important new features or at least
bi-annual FAIRmat events should trigger a minor release.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Major releases require
more involved planning, data migration, and respective instructions and assistance to
NOMAD (Oasis) users.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: They are also political.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
Therefore, they do not a have a regular
schedule.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
With a one `develop` branch Git strategy, there might be necessary exceptions to
regular patch releases.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
In general, new features should be protected by feature switches,
and should not be an issue.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text: However, major refactorings that might effect multiple components are hard to hide behind a feature switch.

title: How to release a new NOMAD version
text:
In such cases, the release schedule might be
put on hold for another month or two.

title: How to extend the search
text: NOMAD uses Elasticsearch as the underlying search engine.

title: How to extend the search
text:
The respective indices
are automatically populated during processing and other NOMAD operations. The indices
are built from some of the archive information of each entry. These are mostly the
sections `metadata` (ids, user metadata, other "administrative" and "internal" metadata)
and `results` (a summary of all extracted (meta)data).

title: How to extend the search
text:
However, these sections are not
indexed verbatim.

title: How to extend the search
text:
What exactly and how it is indexed is determined by the Metainfo
and the `elasticsearch` Metainfo extension.

title: How to extend the search
text:
Here is the definition of `results.material.elements` as an example:

```python
class Material(MSection):
    ...
    elements = Quantity(
        type=MEnum(chemical_symbols),
        shape=["0..*"],
        default=[],
        description='Names of the different elements present in the structure.',
        a_elasticsearch=[
            Elasticsearch(material_type, many_all=True),
            Elasticsearch(suggestion="simple")
        ]
    )
```

Extensions are denoted with the `a_` prefix as in `a_elasticsearch`. Since extensions can have all kinds of values, the `elasticsearch` extension is rather
complex and uses the `Elasticsearch` class.

title: How to extend the search
text: There can be multiple values.

title: How to extend the search
text:
Each `Elasticsearch` instance configures a different part
of the index.

title: How to extend the search
text: This means that the same quantity can be indexed multiple time.

title: How to extend the search
text:
For example,
if you need a text- and a keyword-based search for the same data. Here
is a version of the `metadata.mainfile` definition as another example:

```python
mainfile = metainfo.Quantity(
    type=str, categories=[MongoEntryMetadata, MongoSystemMetadata],
    description='The path to the mainfile from the root directory of the uploaded files',
    a_elasticsearch=[
        Elasticsearch(_es_field='keyword'),
        Elasticsearch(
            mapping=dict(type='text', analyzer=path_analyzer.to_dict()),
            field='path', _es_field='')
    ]
)
```

title: How to extend the search
text:
The first (optional) argument for `Elasticsearch` determines where the data is indexed. There are three principle places:

- the entry index (`entry_type`, default)
- the materials index (`material_type`)
- the entries within the materials index (`material_entry_type`)

title: How to extend the search
text:
This is the default and is used even if another (additional) value is given. All data
is put into the entry index.

title: How to extend the search
text:
This is a separate index from the entry index and contains aggregated material
information. Each document in this index represents a material. We use a hash over some
material properties (elements, system type, symmetry) to define what a material is and
which entries belong to which material. Some parts of the material documents contain the material information that is always
the same for all entries of this material.

title: How to extend the search
text: Examples are elements, formulas, symmetry.

title: How to extend the search
text:
The materials index also contains entry-specific information that allows to filter
materials for the existence of entries with certain criteria.

title: How to extend the search
text:
Examples are publish status,
user metadata, used method, or property data.

title: How to extend the search
text:
In principle, all quantities could be added to the index, but for convention and
simplicity, only quantities defined in the sections `metadata` and `results` should be
added.

title: How to extend the search
text:
This means that if you want to add custom quantities from your parser, for example,
you will also need to customize the results normalizer to copy or reference parsed data.

title: How to extend the search
text: The search API does not have to change.

title: How to extend the search
text:
It automatically supports all quantities with the
`elasticsearch` extension. The keys that you can use in the API are the Metainfo paths of
the respective quantities, e.g. `results.material.elements` or `mainfile` (note that the
`metadata.` prefix is always omitted). If there are multiple `elasticsearch` annotations
for the same quantity, all but one define a `field` parameter, which is added to the
quantity path, e.g. `mainfile.path`.

title: How to extend the search
text:
!!! warning "Attention"
        Coming soon ...

title: How to get started in development
section: How to get started in development
text: This is a step-by-step guide to get started with NOMAD development.

title: How to get started in development
section: How to get started in development
text:
You will clone
all sources, set up a *Python* and *Node.js* environment, install all necessary dependencies,
run the infrastructure in development mode, learn to run the test suites, and set up
*Visual Studio Code* for NOMAD development.

title: How to get started in development
section: How to get started in development
text: This is not about working with the NOMAD Python package `nomad-lab`.

title: How to get started in development
section: How to get started in development
text:
You can find its
documentation [here](../programmatic/pythonlib.md).

title: How to get started in development
text: If you're planning on developing the core `nomad` package alongside other plugins, consider using the `nomad-distro-dev` setup as described at the end of this page.

title: How to get started in development
text:
If not already done, you should clone NOMAD. If you have an account at the
[MPDCF Gitlab](https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/){:target="_blank"}, you can clone with the SSH URL:

```shell
git clone git@gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de:nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR.git nomad
```

Otherwise, clone using the HTTPS URL:

```shell
git clone https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR.git nomad
```

Then change directory to `nomad`

```shell
cd nomad
```

There are several branches in the repository.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The `master` branch contains the latest released version,
but there is also a `develop` (new features) and `release` branch (hotfixes).

title: How to get started in development
text:
There are also
tags for each version called `vX.X.X`.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Check out the branch you want to work on.

```shell
git checkout develop
```

The development branches are protected and you should create a new branch including your changes.

```shell
git checkout -b <my-branch-name>
```

This branch can be pushed to the repo, and then later may be merged to the relevant branch.

title: How to get started in development
text: The NOMAD code currently requires Python 3.12.

title: How to get started in development
text: You should work in a Python virtual environment.

title: How to get started in development
text: For developers using VSCode, in case you encounter any issues with breakpoints not [triggering](https://github.com/microsoft/debugpy/issues/1284), consider upgrading to py3.12.

title: How to get started in development
text:
If your host machine has an older version installed,
you can use [pyenv](https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv){:target="_blank"} to use Python 3.12 in parallel with your
system's Python.

title: How to get started in development
text: Create a virtual environment.

title: How to get started in development
text:
It allows you
to keep NOMAD and its dependencies separate from your system's Python installation.

title: How to get started in development
text: Make sure that the virtual environment is based on Python 3.12.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Use either the built-in `venv` module (see example) or [virtualenv](https://pypi.org/project/virtualenv/){:target="_blank"}.

```shell
python3 -m venv .pyenv
source .pyenv/bin/activate
```

title: How to get started in development
text:
If you are a conda user, there is an equivalent, but you have to install `pip` and the
right Python version while creating the environment.

```shell
conda create --name nomad_env pip python=3.12
conda activate nomad_env
```

To install libmagick for Conda, you can use (other channels might also work):

```shell
conda install -c conda-forge --name nomad_env libmagic
```

title: How to get started in development
text:
Make sure you have the most recent version of `pip`:

```shell
pip install --upgrade pip
```

title: How to get started in development
section: Install missing system libraries (e.g. on Windows, macOS)
text:
Even though the NOMAD infrastructure is written in Python, there are C libraries
required by some of our Python dependencies.

title: How to get started in development
section: Install missing system libraries (e.g. on Windows, macOS)
text:
Specifically, the libmagic library,
which allows determining the MIME type of files, and the hdf5 library, which is
essential for handling HDF5 files, must be installed on most Unix/Linux systems.

title: How to get started in development
section: Install missing system libraries (e.g. on Windows, macOS)
text: The absence of these libraries can lead to issues during installation or runtime.

title: How to get started in development
section: Install missing system libraries (e.g. on Windows, macOS)
text:
For macOS:

```bash
brew install hdf5 libmagic file-formula
```

For Windows (pre-compiled binaries for `hdf5` are included in the dependencies):

-libmagic: We include python-magic-bin as a dependency for Windows users.

title: How to get started in development
section: Install missing system libraries (e.g. on Windows, macOS)
text:
If you encounter an error such as NameError: name '_compressions' is not defined, try uninstalling and reinstalling the library:

```bash
pip uninstall python-magic-bin
pip install python-magic-bin
```

You can confirm that the magic library is correctly installed by running:

```bash
python -c "import magic"
```

title: How to get started in development
text:
The following command can be used to install NOMAD.

```shell
./scripts/setup_dev_env.sh
```

??? note "Installation details"
    Here is more detailed rundown of the installation steps.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Previous build is cleaned:

    ```shell
    rm -rf nomad/app/static/docs
    rm -rf nomad/app/static/gui
    rm -rf site
    ```

    We use [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) to install the packages.

title: How to get started in development
text: You can install uv using `pip install uv`.

title: How to get started in development
text: Next we install the `nomad` package itself (including all extras).

title: How to get started in development
text:
The `-e`
    option will install NOMAD with symbolic links that allow you to change
    the code without having to reinstall after each change. The -c flag restricts the installation of dependencies to the versions specified in the provided requirements file, ensuring that only those versions are installed.

    ```shell
    uv pip install -e .[parsing,infrastructure,dev] -c requirements-dev.txt
    ```

    Install "default" plugins. TODO: This can be removed once we have proper proper distribution
    ```shell
    uv pip install -r requirements-plugins.txt
    ```

    Next we build the documentation.

    ```shell
    sh scripts/generate_docs_artifacts.sh
    mkdocs build
    mkdir -p nomad/app/static/docs
    cp -r site/* nomad/app/static/docs
    ```

    The NOMAD GUI requires a static `.env` file, which can be generated with:

    ```shell
    python -m nomad.cli dev gui-env > gui/.env.development
    ```

    This file includes some of the server details needed so that the
    GUI can make the initial connection properly. If, for example, you change the server
    address in your NOMAD configuration file, it will be necessary to regenerate
    this `.env` file. In production this file will be overridden. In addition, you have to do some more steps to prepare your working copy to run
all the tests, see below.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You need to install [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/){:target="_blank"}. Docker nowadays comes with Docker Compose (`docker compose`) built-in. Prior, you needed to
install the standalone [Docker Compose (`docker-compose`)](https://docs.docker.com/compose/install/){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to get started in development
text:
To run NOMAD, some 3rd party services are needed

- Elasticsearch: NOMAD's search and analytics engine
- MongoDB: used to store processing state
- RabbitMQ: a task queue used to distribute work in a cluster

All 3rd party services should be run via `docker compose` (see below).

title: How to get started in development
text:
Keep in mind that `docker compose` configures all services in a way that mirrors
the configuration of the Python code in `nomad/config.py` and the GUI config in
`gui/.env.development`.

title: How to get started in development
text: The default virtual memory for Elasticsearch will likely be too low.

title: How to get started in development
text:
On Linux, you can run the following command as root:

```shell
sysctl -w vm.max_map_count=262144
```

To set this value permanently, see [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/vm-max-map-count.html){:target="_blank"}. Then you can run all services with:

```shell
cd ops/docker-compose/infrastructure
docker compose up -d elastic mongo rabbitmq
cd ../../..
```

If your system almost ran out of disk space, Elasticsearch enforces a read-only index block ([read more](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/6.2/disk-allocator.html){:target="_blank"}), but
after clearing up the disk space you need to reset it manually using the following command:

```shell
curl -XPUT -H "Content-Type: application/json" http://localhost:9200/_all/_settings -d '{"index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete": false}'
```

Note that the Elasticsearch service has a known problem in quickly hitting the
virtual memory limits of your OS. If you experience issues with the
Elasticsearch container not running correctly or crashing, try increasing the
virtual memory limits as shown [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/vm-max-map-count.html){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to get started in development
text: To shut down everything, just `ctrl-c` the running output.

title: How to get started in development
text:
If you started everything
in *deamon* mode (`-d`) use:

```shell
docker compose down
```

Usually these services are used only by NOMAD, but sometimes you also
need to check something or do some manual steps.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You can access MongoDB and Elasticsearch
via your preferred tools.

title: How to get started in development
text: Just make sure to use the right ports.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Before you run NOMAD for development purposes, you should configure it to use the test
realm of our user management system. By default, NOMAD will use the `fairdi_nomad_prod` realm. Create a `nomad.yaml` file in the root folder:

```yaml
keycloak:
  realm_name: fairdi_nomad_test
```

You might also want to exclude some of the default plugins, or only include the plugins
you'll need. Especially plugins with slower start-up and import times due to instantiation
of large schemas (e.g. nexus create couple thousand definitions for 70+ applications) can
often be excluded:

```yaml
plugins:
  exclude:
    - parsers/nexus
```

Note that this will lead to [failing tests](#backend-tests) for the excluded plugins.

title: How to get started in development
text:
NOMAD consists of the NOMAD app/API, a Celery worker, and the GUI. You can run the app and the worker with
the NOMAD CLI. These commands will run the services and display their log output.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You should open
them in separate shells as they run continuously. They will not watch code changes and
you have to restart manually.

```shell
nomad admin run app
```

```shell
nomad admin run worker
```

Or both together in one process:

```shell
nomad admin run appworker
```

On macOS you might run into multiprocessing errors.

title: How to get started in development
text: That can be solved as described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50168647/multiprocessing-causes-python-to-crash-and-gives-an-error-may-have-been-in-progr){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The app will run at port 8000 by default. Depending on your preference, it is also possible to run the app and worker together with a python script.

```bash
python3 scripts/run_dev_env.py
```

The `scripts/run_dev_env.py` enables develop mode by default such that only one worker is used for FastAPI and Celery.

title: How to get started in development
text: This helps save resource usage, which is often useful for development purposes.

title: How to get started in development
text: There are more configurations available.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You can see them by running the following.

```shell
nomad admin run --help
```

To run the worker directly with Celery, do (from the root)

```shell
celery -A nomad.processing worker -l info
```

If you run the GUI on its own (e.g. with the React dev server below), you also need to start
the app manually. The GUI and its dependencies run on [Node.js](https://nodejs.org){:target="_blank"} and
the [Yarn](https://yarnpkg.com/){:target="_blank"} dependency manager. Read their documentation on how to
install them for your platform.

```shell
cd gui
yarn
yarn start
```

Note that the current codebase requires Node.js version 20.

title: How to get started in development
text: NOMAD also has a built-in JupyterHub that is used to launch remote tools (e.g.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Jupyter
notebooks).

title: How to get started in development
text:
To run JupyterHub, some additional configuration might be necessary.

```yaml
north:
  hub_connect_ip: 'host.docker.internal'
  jupyterhub_crypt_key: '<crypt key>'
```

On Windows system, you might have to activate further specific functionality:

```yaml
north:
  hub_connect_ip: 'host.docker.internal'
  hub_connect_url: 'http://host.docker.internal:8081'
  windows: true
  jupyterhub_crypt_key: '<crypt key>'
```

- If you are not on Linux, you need to configure how JupyterHub can reach your host
  network from docker containers. For Windows and macOS you need to set `hub_connect_ip`
  to `host.docker.internal`. For Linux you can leave it out and use the default
  `172.17.0.1`, unless you changed your docker configuration.

- You have to generate a `crypt key` with `openssl rand -hex 32`.

- You might need to install
  [configurable-http-proxy](https://github.com/jupyterhub/configurable-http-proxy){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to get started in development
text: The `configurable-http-proxy` comes as a Node.js package.

title: How to get started in development
text:
See
[Node.js](https://nodejs.org){:target="_blank"} for how to install `npm`.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The proxy can be globally
installed with:

```shell
npm install -g configurable-http-proxy
```

JupyterHub is a separate application.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You can run JuypterHub similar to the other part:

```shell
nomad admin run hub
```

To run JupyterHub directly, do (from the root)

```shell
jupyterhub -f nomad/jupyterhub_config.py --port 9000
```

title: How to get started in development
text:
To run the tests some additional settings and files are necessary that are not part
of the codebase.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You have to provide static files to serve the docs and NOMAD distribution:

```shell
./scripts/generate_docs_artifacts.sh
rm -rf site && mkdocs build && mv site nomad/app/static/docs
```

You need to have the infrastructure partially running: `elastic`, `mongo`, `rabbitmq`.

title: How to get started in development
text: The rest should be mocked or provided by the tests.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Make sure that you do not run any
worker, as they will fight for tasks in the queue.

title: How to get started in development
text:
To start the infrastructure and run the
tests, use:

```shell
cd ops/docker-compose/infrastructure
docker compose up -d elastic mongo rabbitmq
cd ../../..
pytest -sv tests
```

!!! note
    Some of these tests will fail because a few large files are not included in the Git
    repository.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You may ignore these for local testing, they are still checked by the
    CI/CD pipeline:

    ```text
    FAILED tests/archive/test_archive.py::test_read_springer - AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'seek'
    FAILED tests/normalizing/test_material.py::test_material_bulk - assert None
    FAILED tests/normalizing/test_system.py::test_springer_normalizer - IndexError: list index out of range
    ```

    If you excluded plugins in your [NOMAD config](#nomadyaml), then those tests
    will also fail.

title: How to get started in development
text:
We use Ruff and Mypy to maintain code quality. Additionally, we recommend installing the Ruff [plugins](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/integrations/){:target="_blank"} for your code editor to streamline the process. To execute Ruff and Mypy from the command line, you can utilize the following command:

```shell
nomad dev qa --skip-tests
```

We use ruff as a linter and as an autoformatter. If you only want to lint your code, you can run:

```shell
ruff check .
```

To format your code you can run:

```shell
ruff format .
```

To run all tests and code QA:

```shell
nomad dev qa
```

This mimics the tests and checks that the GitLab CI/CD will perform.

title: How to get started in development
text: To ensure that all tests are independent despite reusing the same queue and workers, the `worker` fixture cleans up all running tasks after the test.

title: How to get started in development
text: This involves by default a timeout of one second, which accumulates over all tests using that fixture. In one local development environment this made a difference of about 14 min vs. 7 min without the timeout. If you want to speed up your local testing, you can use `pytest --celery-inspect-timeout 0.1` to shorten the timeout to a tenth of a second.

title: How to get started in development
text: Be aware that this might leave tasks running, which can affect later tests.

title: How to get started in development
text: You may want to run only tests that use a specific fixture, e.g. if you are editing that fixture. For example, to run only tests with the `worker` fixture, use `pytest --fixture-filters worker`.

title: How to get started in development
text: If you list more than one, all of them must be requested by the test to be included.

title: How to get started in development
text: You can also negate a fixture by prefixing its name with `!`.

title: How to get started in development
text: For example, to run all tests that do not depend on the `worker` fixture, use `pytest --fixture-filters '!worker'` (quotes are needed for `!`).

title: How to get started in development
text: Note that if `test1` depends on `fixture1`, and `fixture1` depends on `fixture2`, then `test1` also depends on `fixture2`, even though it was not explicitly listed as a parameter.

title: How to get started in development
text:
We use
[`testing-library`](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/intro/){:target="_blank"}
to implement our GUI tests and `testing-library` itself uses
[`Jest`](https://jestjs.io/){:target="_blank"} to run the tests.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Tests are written in `*.spec.js`
files that accompany the implementation.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Tests should focus on functionality,
not on implementation details: `testing-library` is designed to enforce this kind
of testing.

!!! note

    When testing HTML output, the elements are rendered using
    [jsdom](https://github.com/jsdom/jsdom){:target="_blank"}: this is not completely identical
    to using an actual browser (e.g. does not support WebGL), but in practice
    is realistic enough for the majority of the test.

title: How to get started in development
text:
We have adopted a `pytest`-like structure for organizing the test utilities:
each source code folder may contain a `conftest.spec.js` file that contains
utilities that are relevant for testing the code in that particular folder.

title: How to get started in development
text:
These utilities can usually be placed into the following categories:

- Custom renders: When testing React components, the
  [`render`](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/api/#render){:target="_blank"} function
  is used to display them on the test DOM.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Typically your components require
  some parts of the infrastructure to work properly, which is achieved by
  wrapping your component with other components that provide a context. Custom
  render functions can do this automatically for you, e.g. the default render
  as exported from `src/components/conftest.spec.js` wraps your components with an
  infrastructure that is very similar to the production app.

title: How to get started in development
text:
See
  [here](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/setup/#custom-render){:target="_blank"}
  for more information.

- Custom queries: See
  [here](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/setup/#add-custom-queries){:target="_blank"}
  for more information.

- Custom expects: These are reusable functions that perform actual tests using
  the `expect` function.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Whenever the same tests are performed by several
  `*.spec.js` files, you should formalize these common tests into an
  `expect*` function and place it in a relevant `conftest.spec.js` file.

title: How to get started in development
text: Often your components will need to communicate with the API during tests.

title: How to get started in development
text:
One
should generally avoid using manually created mocks for the API traffic, and
instead prefer using API responses that originate from an actual API call
during testing.

title: How to get started in development
text:
It requires a lot of work to manually create mocks
and keep them up-to-date, and true integration tests are impossible
to perform with live communication with an API.

title: How to get started in development
text:
In order to simplify the API
communication during testing, you can use the `startAPI`+`closeAPI` functions, that
will prepare the API traffic for you. A simple example could look like this:

```javascript
import React from 'react'
import { waitFor } from '@testing-library/dom'
import { startAPI, closeAPI, screen } from '../../conftest'
import { renderSearchEntry, expectInputHeader } from '../conftest'

test('periodic table shows the elements retrieved through the API', async () => {
  startAPI('<state_name>', '<snapshot_name>')
  renderSearchEntry(...)
  expect(...)
  closeAPI()
})
```

Here the important parameters are:

- `<state_name>`: Specifies an initial backend configuration for this test.

title: How to get started in development
text:
These
  states are defined as Python functions that are stored in
  `nomad-FAIR/tests/states`, example given below.

title: How to get started in development
text:
These functions may, for example,
  prepare several uploads entries, datasets, etc. for the test.

- `<snapshot_name>`: Specifies a filepath for reading/recording pre-recorded API
  traffic.

title: How to get started in development
text:
An example of a simple test state could look like this:

```python
from nomad import infrastructure
from nomad.utils import create_uuid
from nomad.utils.exampledata import ExampleData

def search():
    infrastructure.setup()
    main_author = infrastructure.user_management.get_user(username="test")
    data = ExampleData(main_author=main_author)
    upload_id = create_uuid()
    data.create_upload(upload_id=upload_id, published=True, embargo_length=0)
    data.create_entry(
        upload_id=upload_id,
        entry_id=create_uuid(),
        mainfile="test_content/test_entry/mainfile.json",
        results={
            "material": {"elements": ["C", "H"]},
            "method": {},
            "properties": {}
        }
    )
    data.save()
```

When running in the online mode (see below), this function will be executed in
order to prepare the application backend.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The `closeAPI` function will handle
cleaning the test state between successive `startAPI` calls: it will completely
wipe out MongoDB, Elasticsearch and the upload files.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The tests can be run in two different modes. *Offline testing* uses
pre-recorded files to mock the API traffic during testing.

title: How to get started in development
text:
This allows one to
run tests more quickly without a server. During *online testing*, the tests
perform calls to a running server where a test state has been prepared. This
mode can be used to perform integration tests but also to record the snapshot
files needed by the offline testing. Before running tests, ensure that the GUI artifacts are up-to-date:

    ```sh
    ./scripts/generate_gui_test_artifacts.sh
    ```

    As snapshot tests do not connect to the server, the artifacts cannot be
    fetched dynamically from the server and static files need to be used instead. Note that you should not push these files to Git: the CI/CD pipeline will
    automatically generate them.

title: How to get started in development
text:
This is the way our CI pipeline runs the tests and should be used locally, e.g.
whenever you wish to reproduce pipeline errors or when your tests do not
involve any API traffic.

title: How to get started in development
text: Run `yarn test` to run the whole suite, or `yarn test [<filename>]` to run a specific test.

title: How to get started in development
text:
When you wish to record API traffic for offline testing, or to perform
integration tests, you will need to have a server running with the correct
configuration.

title: How to get started in development
text:
To do this, follow these steps:

1.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Have the docker infrastructure running: `docker compose up -d`

2.

title: How to get started in development
text:
Have the `nomad appworker` running with the config found in `gui/tests/nomad.yaml`:
`export NOMAD_CONFIG=gui/tests/nomad.yaml && nomad admin run appworker`

3. Activate the correct Python virtual environment before running the tests
with Yarn (Yarn will run the Python functions that prepare the state).

4. Run the tests with `yarn test-record [<filename>]` if you wish to record a
snapshot file, or `yarn test-integration [<filename>]` if you want the
perform the test without any recording.

title: How to get started in development
text: To create a custom Oasis image (e.g. with custom plugins), you could use the [nomad-distro-template](https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/nomad-distro-template) by clicking the [`Use this template` button](https://github.com/new?template_name=nomad-distro-template&template_owner=FAIRmat-NFDI).

title: How to get started in development
text: For detailed instructions on building and deploying custom Oasis images, please refer to the documentation in that repository.

title: How to get started in development
text:
The documentation section for development guidelines (see below) provide details on how
the code is organized, tested, formatted, and documented.

title: How to get started in development
text:
To help you meet these
guidelines, we recommend to use a proper IDE for development and ditch any Vim/Emacs
(mal-)practices.

title: How to get started in development
text:
We strongly recommend that all developers use *Visual Studio Code (VS Code)*. (This is a
completely different product than *Visual Studio*.) It is available for free
for all major platforms [here](https://code.visualstudio.com/download){:target="_blank"}.

title: How to get started in development
text:
You should launch and run VS Code directly from the project's root directory. The source
code already contains settings for VS Code in the `.vscode` directory. The settings
contain the same setup for style checks, linter, etc. that is also used in our CI/CD
pipelines. In order to actually use the these features, you have to make sure that they
are enabled in your own User settings:

```json
"python.linting.mypyEnabled": true,
"python.testing.pytestEnabled": true,
"[python]": {
  "editor.formatOnSave": true,
  "editor.defaultFormatter": "charliermarsh.ruff"
}
```

The settings also include a few launch configuration for VS Code's debugger. You can create
your own launch configs in `.vscode/launch.json` (also in `.gitignore`). The settings expect that you have installed a Python environment at `.pyenv` as
described in this tutorial (see above).

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text:
When developing `nomad` alongside multiple plugins, managing different repositories and installations can become complex.
`nomad-distro-dev` simplifies this by allowing you to develop `nomad` and multiple plugins in one environment.

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text:
Quick Setup

1.

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text:
Fork the [nomad-distro-dev](https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/nomad-distro-dev) repository on GitHub.
2.

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text:
Follow the setup steps listed in the README of the fork.
3.

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text: Once configured, you'll be able to use a single VSCode window to work on nomad and all your plugins.

title: How to get started in development
section: `nomad-distro-dev`: Development environment for the core `nomad` package and NOMAD plugins
text: The setup should generally work without any issues, but there are some troubleshooting tips on this page that you can come back to if needed.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text:
A [GraphQL](https://graphql.org/)-like API is implemented to allow flexible and accurate data fetching.

??? note "technical details"
    It is categorized as a `GraphQL`-**like** API implemented within the `REST` style framework `FastAPI`. Because `GraphQL` requires static, explicitly defined schemas ahead of time while `NOMAD` supports data with dynamic schema,
    it cannot be implemented directly using existing `GraphQL` tools.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: As a result, there are no GUI tools available unfortunately.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: In general, `REST` is good for simple data fetching, while the project gets more complex, APIs in REST style become more complex and less flexible.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text:
When building a complex page, often a single request is not enough, and multiple requests are needed to fetch all the necessary data.
`GraphQL` aims to solve this over-/under-fetching problem so that both performance and bandwidth can be optimized.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: You walk into a restaurant, go through the menu, and order several dishes at once. The kitchen prepares all the dishes and serves them to you, nothing more, nothing less.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: This is effectively how `GraphQL` works.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: In `NOMAD`, we mimic this behaviour with a `GraphQL`-like API.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: The only endpoint involved is `/graph/query`.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: All the magic happens there.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: But before that, we shall first explain some basic concepts.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: Imagine there is an example upload with the upload ID `example_upload_id`. The metadata of this upload is stored in `MongoDB`.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: In our mental model, it is possible to organize all uploads in a tree structure, under the root `uploads`.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: This better helps us to define interactions among different data structures (to be explained later).

title: The Graph(-style) API
The Graph(-style) API
text: An instance of `RequestConfig` shall be attached to each required field.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text:

title: The Graph(-style) API
The Graph(-style) API
text:
As of this writing, there are a few existing data resources (called `Documents`) stored in `MongoDB`:

1. `uploads` (stored in MongoDB): The metadata of an upload, including, `upload_id`, `upload_name`, `main_author`, etc.
2. `entries` (stored in MongoDB): The metadata of an entry, including, `entry_id`, `entry_create_time`, `mainfile`, etc.
3. `datasets` (stored in MongoDB): The metadata of a dataset, including, `dataset_id`, `dataset_name`, `user_id`, etc.
4. `groups` (stored in MongoDB): The metadata of a user group, including, `owner`, `members`, etc.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: One can apply the same logic to fetch data from these structures.

title: The Graph(-style) API
The Graph(-style) API
text: This concludes the basic usage of the `/graph/query` endpoint.

title: The Graph(-style) API
text: There are other resources to explore, which will be explained in the subsequent pages.

title: The Graph Structure
text: In the previous page, we explained how to fetch data from existing data structures.

title: The Graph Structure
text: This along does not make it a 'graph' because we only have isolated 'nodes'.

title: The Graph Structure
text: To navigate from one data structure to another, we need to introduce 'edges' to link 'nodes' together.

title: The Graph Structure
text: The data structures in `NOMAD` are inherently linked together.

title: The Graph Structure
text:
For example, an upload is a collection of entries, an entry corresponding to an archive, a user group is a collection of users,
each user may be the owner of several uploads, etc.

title: The Graph Structure
text: Thus, it is natural to link these data structures together via **special tokens**.

title: The Graph Structure
The Graph Structure
text: It shall be noted that those 'special tokens' do not exist in the original documents stored in `MongoDB`.

title: The Graph Structure
text: They are defined by the graph API to establish the relationships between the data structures.

title: The Graph Structure
text: Imagine we start with an upload with a known ID `example_upload_id`, and we want to find all entries that belong to this upload.

title: The Graph Structure
text: How can we achieve this without knowing the exact entry IDs a priori?

title: The Graph Structure
The Graph Structure
text: It won't work in archives, the corresponding metainfo (definitions), and alike.

title: The Graph Structure
text:
The request configuration allows one to perform fuzzy queries to further filter data before fetching, via the `query` and `pagination` fields.

```py hl_lines="4-24"
class RequestConfig(BaseModel):
    # ... other fields omitted for brevity ...

    pagination: None | dict = Field(
        None,
        description="""
        The pagination configuration used for MongoDB search.

title: The Graph Structure
text: This setting does not propagate to its children.

title: The Graph Structure
text: For Token.ENTRIES, Token.UPLOADS and Token.DATASETS, different validation rules apply.

title: The Graph Structure
text:
Please refer to `DatasetPagination`, `UploadProcDataPagination`, `MetadataPagination` for details.
        """,
    )
    query: None | dict = Field(
        None,
        description="""
        The query configuration used for either mongo or elastic search.

title: The Graph Structure
text: This setting does not propagate to its children.

title: The Graph Structure
text: It can only be defined at the root levels including Token.ENTRIES, Token.UPLOADS and 'm_datasets'. For Token.ENTRIES, the query is used in elastic search.

title: The Graph Structure
text: It must comply with `WithQuery`.

title: The Graph Structure
text: For Token.UPLOADS, the query is used in mongo search.

title: The Graph Structure
text: It must comply with `UploadProcDataQuery`.

title: The Graph Structure
text: For Token.DATASETS, the query is used in mongo search.

title: The Graph Structure
text: It must comply with `DatasetQuery`.

title: The Graph Structure
text: For Token.GROUPS, the query is used in mongo search.

title: The Graph Structure
text:
It must comply with `UserGroupQuery`.
        """,
    )

    # ... other fields omitted for brevity ...
```

To instruct the server to perform a query, one need to attach a request config with valid `query` and `pagination` (optional) with the `m_request` key under the **root** level. The **root** level is the top-level following the **special tokens**, such as `uploads`, `entries`, etc.

title: The Graph Structure
text: Combined with fuzzy fetching, one can perform filter and fetch.

title: The Graph Structure
The Graph Structure
text: Those models are used in conventional REST endpoints, and one can find more details in the corresponding documentation.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: Apart from the core functionalities that cover MongoDB database and archive data, there are additional resources that can be fetched.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: In this page, we briefly summarize these functionalities.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: As all data are associated with the corresponding users, in our mental model, uploads, entries, datasets, etc., are connected to a user node.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: This can be done conveniently by using the special token `me`.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
Miscellaneous Functionalities
Miscellaneous Functionalities
Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: And it is likely that the desired information can be directly fetched from `Elasticsearch`.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: In this case, there is no need to navigate to the `MongoDB` database. However, if the request needs to access archive, it has to use `entry -> archive` path if starting from `search`.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text:
As each entry corresponds to a main file, and each upload corresponds to a folder, the graph API also provides a convenient way to fetch file information, similar to the `ls` command in Linux. There are two ways to access the file system.

1.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
text:
Inside an upload, use the token `files`.
2. Inside an entry, use the token `mainfile`.

title: Miscellaneous Functionalities
Miscellaneous Functionalities
text: This is particularly useful when listing files in an upload, as there can be many files.

title: Querying Archive
text: In the previous page, we explained how to fetch data from different data resources (mainly `MongoDB` documents).

title: Querying Archive
text: They are mostly flat (with only a few levels of nesting) and relatively easy to query and fetch.

title: Querying Archive
text: The very idea can be extended to fetching archives that are stored on the file system.

title: Querying Archive
Querying Archive
text: We will introduce other directives later.

title: Querying Archive
text: The above query will return the contents of the target archive.

title: Querying Archive
Querying Archive
Querying Archive
Querying Archive
Querying Archive
text: There is one exception. If the value is a primitive (like a string, number, boolean, etc.), it is always returned as is. This is because generating internal reference strings for primitive values makes little sense and often has a negative impact on performance.

title: Querying Archive
text: Some archives may contain large lists or dictionaries, and not all of them may be needed.

title: Querying Archive
Querying Archive
Querying Archive
Querying Archive
text: Archives may contain references that point to some other locations in the archive, or even to other entries. Conceptually it is similar to the soft links in file systems.

title: Querying Archive
text: By using a default request configuration, references will be returned as they are.
